q: The burning wood in the air releases heat and light energies.
Photosynthesis is the chemical reaction that occurs when plants use energy from the sun, carbon dioxide, and water to synthesise the food that the plant needs to survive.
Except for the energy produced from nuclear reactors, all of man’s energy resources are chemicals or involve the utilisation of energy through chemical reactions.
Energy changes are central to chemical reactions and understanding the role of energy in chemistry provides for our daily life.
Whenever a chemical reaction occurs, energy is usually transferred to or from the surroundings.
Changing states of matter involves physical changes but does not involve energy changes.
Mothball and dry ice sublime without melting as examples of changing states around us in everyday life.
Energy can neither be created nor destroyed as stated in the Law of Conservation of Energy.
Energy can change from one form to another with a net loss or gain of energy depending on the transformation.
An electric motor transforms electrical energy into kinetic energy.
A hair dryer transforms electrical energy into chemical energy.
A battery transforms chemical energy into electrical energy.
An automobile engine transforms chemical energy into mechanical energy.
A solar cell transforms light energy into electrical energy.
Whether a chemical reaction absorbs or releases energy, the overall energy is not changed during the reaction.
Chemical energy is the energy that is stored in the bonds of chemical compounds such as atoms and molecules.
Chemical energy is the source of most of the energy that humans need to function.
Plants store chemical energy in carbohydrates formed during photosynthesis.
Whenever a chemical reaction takes place between reactants to form products, there is always an energy change usually in the form of heat.
Chemical reactions often involve changes in energy due to the breaking and formation of bonds.
Energy change is due to the difference in the amounts of stored chemical energy between the reactant and the product particles in a chemical reaction.
In an instant cold pack, energy is released to break bonds and energy is absorbed when bonds are formed.
Chemical reactions provide electricity which is the result of interactions between ions in an electrolyte.
A process that can take place on its own accord is called spontaneous.
Non-spontaneous processes need the energy to take place.
Adding a solution of dilute sulphuric acid to a solution of sodium hydroxide is an example of a non-spontaneous reaction.
The reaction between magnesium and oxygen requires energy to initiate the reaction.
Iron rusting is exothermic and spontaneous.
When the ice melts at room temperature, it is a spontaneous and endothermic process.
An exothermic reaction is known as a change where heat is being released to the surroundings by the reacting system.
The combustion of a fuel and the neutralisation reaction are examples of endothermic reactions.
In exothermic reactions, the energy of the product is lower than the energy of the reactants because energy has been released during the reaction.
When ammonium chloride is dissolved in water, the flask becomes quite cold to the touch because heat is being absorbed from the surroundings.
An endothermic reaction is one which absorbs heat from the surroundings.
The thermal decomposition of limestone is an example of an exothermic reaction.
In endothermic reactions, the energy of the products is higher than the energy of the reactants because energy has been absorbed during the reaction.
Energy changes in chemical reactions are caused by the making and breaking of chemical bonds.
In a chemical reaction, bonds in the reactants must be broken first, which is an endothermic process.
Bond making is an exothermic process because energy is released when new bonds form.
If the energy absorbed in bond breaking is less than the energy given out in bond making, the reaction is exothermic.
If the energy absorbed in bond breaking is more than the energy given out in bond making, the reaction is endothermic.
A system is the specific part of the universe that contains the reaction or process which is studied.
Surroundings are everything in the universe other than the system.
The total chemical energy of a substance is called its enthalpy (or heat content).
The symbol $H$ is used to represent enthalpy in chemical reactions.
The change in enthalpy describes the amount of heat transferred at constant pressure during a chemical reaction.
Heat changes in chemical reactions can be measured by using a calorimeter.
The heat absorbed or released in a process occurring at constant pressure is called the enthalpy change, symbolised as $\Delta H$.
The unit of enthalpy change is kilojoule per mole ($\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$).
The $\Delta H$ value gives the amount of energy that is absorbed or released per mole of the product formed.
Enthalpy change can be expressed as $\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$.
For an endothermic reaction, the total energy of the products is greater than that of the reactants, so $\Delta H$ is positive.
For an exothermic reaction, the total energy of the products is less than that of the reactants, so $\Delta H$ is negative.
The enthalpy of a reaction is independent of the conditions under which the reaction is carried out.
The standard temperature for energy changes is $25 ^\circ\text{C}$ or $298 \text{K}$.
The standard pressure for energy changes is $1 \text{atm}$ which is equivalent to $101 \text{kPa}$.
A thermochemical equation is a balanced chemical equation that includes the physical states of all reactants and products and the standard enthalpy change.
In thermochemical equations, the physical state of a liquid is indicated by the letter (l) in brackets.
The standard enthalpy change of a reaction ($\Delta H_{\text{r}}^{\theta}$) is the enthalpy change when molar quantities of reactants react to give products under standard conditions.
The standard enthalpy change of formation of a compound ($\Delta H_{\text{f}}^{\theta}$) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a compound is formed from its elements under standard conditions.
The formation of a compound must always be an endothermic process.
The standard enthalpy change of formation of an element in its standard state is zero.
Graphite is considered the most stable form of carbon when writing thermochemical equations.
Standard enthalpy change of combustion ($\Delta H_{\text{c}}^{\theta}$) is the enthalpy change when one mole of a substance is burnt completely in oxygen under standard conditions.
Combustion reactions are always exothermic and the enthalpy changes are always negative.

Standard enthalpy change of neutralisation ($\Delta H_{\text{n}}^{\theta}$) is the enthalpy change when solutions of an acid and an alkali react together under standard conditions to produce one mole of water.
The neutralisation reaction is exothermic and the enthalpy change is negative.
Experimentally, the enthalpy change $\Delta H$ is measured directly using a thermometer.
The heat change, $q$, is calculated using the equation $q = mc\Delta T$.
If the measurements of heat change are performed under constant pressure, $\Delta H$ is equal to the $q$ measured.
The enthalpy change of solution, $\Delta H_{\text{sol}}$, of a substance is the enthalpy change when one mole of the substance is dissolved in water.
The specific heat capacity of water, $c$, has a value of $4.18 \text{J g}^{-1} ^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$.
If the final temperature is greater than the initial temperature ($\Delta T$ is positive), the $q$ value will be positive and the reaction is giving off energy.
A bomb calorimeter is used for determining the heat of combustion by igniting a sample in a high pressure of oxygen in a sealed vessel.
Highly accurate values of the standard enthalpy change of combustion can be measured only by a bomb calorimeter.
The specific heat capacity of a liquid is the energy required to raise the temperature of $1 \text{g}$ of liquid by $1 ^\circ\text{C}$.
Hess's law states that the enthalpy change for a chemical reaction is the same, whatever route is taken from reactants to products.
Hess’s law allows the enthalpy change for reactions that are difficult to measure directly to be determined indirectly.
In an enthalpy cycle, the enthalpy change of Route I is equal to the enthalpy changes of Route II.

According to Hess’s law, if there are more than two steps in Route II, the total enthalpy change is the sum of the individual steps: $\Delta H_{\text{r}} = \Delta H_{1} + \Delta H_{2} + \Delta H_{3} + \dots$.
To determine the enthalpy change of reaction from enthalpy changes of formation, reactants and products should be considered with elements written at the bottom.
In a Hess's cycle for formation, arrows point from the elements upwards to the reactants and products.
To determine the enthalpy change of formation from enthalpy changes of combustion, the combustion products (usually carbon dioxide and water) are written at the bottom.
In an enthalpy cycle involving combustion data, arrows point downward from the elements and compounds to the combustion products.
Enthalpy changes are due to the breaking and forming of bonds.
Breaking a bond absorbs energy, making $\Delta H$ positive.
Making a bond releases energy, making $\Delta H$ negative.
The energy needed to make or break bonds is called bond energy or bond enthalpy.
The higher the bond enthalpy, the weaker the chemical bond.
The symbol $E$ is used to represent bond enthalpy.
The bond enthalpy for double and triple bonds refers to a mole of double or triple bonds.
The amount of energy released when new bonds are formed is exactly the same as the amount of energy absorbed when the same type of bond is broken.
Hess’s law can be used to find approximate values of enthalpy change of a reaction using bond enthalpies.
In burning fuels such as coal or propane, an exothermic reaction takes place.
The endothermic reaction takes place during cooking food, baking bread, and boiling water.
Hand warmers produce heat from the endothermic reaction of chemicals.
Automobile manufacturers use Hess’s law to determine how much energy an engine consumes or generates while burning fuels.
A lot of weight loss applications use Hess’s law concept to calculate the number of calories burned during a workout.
In an exothermic reaction, the surrounding temperature is increased.
An endothermic process may be used as a cooling system.
